questions.question: Why is a single central counter often too slow for high-scale edge rate limiting?
options: Because it cannot store more than one key at a time in memory.
Because every request must pay coordination latency and hotspot pressure on the same shared path.
Because token buckets are incompatible with distributed storage.
Because edge gateways are not allowed to make local decisions.
questions.explanation: A single coordination point increases latency and creates hotspots under skewed traffic.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of burst allowance in a rate limiter?
options: It lets traffic briefly exceed the steady rate without treating every spike as abuse.
It permanently doubles the allowed traffic for premium customers.
It removes the need for a shared control plane.
It guarantees no requests will ever be rejected.
questions.explanation: Burst capacity absorbs short spikes while preserving the long-term rate policy.

questions.question: What problem does a local fast path solve in a distributed limiter?
options: It eliminates all coordination and consistency problems completely.
It reduces hot-path latency by serving common decisions locally with bounded staleness.
It allows policy updates to skip versioning.
It guarantees fairness across all regions without shared state.
questions.explanation: Local caching reduces latency, but only within a controlled inconsistency window.

questions.question: Why is explainability valuable in a rate limiting system?
options: It helps operators and customers understand why requests were rejected and how policy behaved.
It is only needed for local development environments.
It allows burst size to be recalculated without policy storage.
It replaces metrics during incidents.
questions.explanation: Without reject explanations, tuning and incident diagnosis become much harder.

questions.question: What trade-off does fail-open introduce during a shared-store outage?
options: It preserves availability but weakens enforcement while degraded mode lasts.
It improves strictness at the cost of higher storage usage.
It removes the need for cache TTLs.
It guarantees equal fairness for all tenants.
questions.explanation: Fail-open keeps traffic flowing, but enforcement becomes less strict while coordination is unavailable.

questions.question: Which metric best helps detect policy drift across the fleet?
options: CPU usage on the gateway process alone.
Policy version skew across edges or POPs.
Only the total number of allowed requests.
Average response body size.
questions.explanation: Version skew directly reveals whether some nodes are enforcing older rules.

questions.question: Why are hot keys dangerous in distributed rate limiting?
options: They concentrate traffic onto a small portion of the fleet and can overload one shard or owner.
They make TLS termination impossible at the edge.
They only matter when keys are stored in SQL databases.
They prevent any kind of burst handling.
questions.explanation: Skew can overwhelm the owner shard even when average fleet load looks normal.

questions.question: What is the clearest reason to choose token bucket over a sliding request log in many interviews?
options: It is simpler to explain, supports bursts naturally, and avoids the memory overhead of storing many timestamps.
It guarantees stronger consistency than any shared counter design.
It requires no time calculations at all.
It makes long-horizon monthly quotas trivial.
questions.explanation: Token bucket is compact, intuitive, and often sufficient for short-window enforcement.
